meta.chapter_title: Debugging a prompt like an editor debugs a draft
meta.chapter_subtitle: Move from rewriting whole prompts to systematically isolating what's broken.
meta.current_level_rubric: Knows that prompts can be revised when output is unsatisfactory
meta.target_level_rubric: Systematically debugs prompts: isolates variables, tests variations, logs results

scenario.kicker: Where you are. Where you're going.
scenario.title: The client email that keeps missing
scenario.narrative: A financial wellness brand has asked you to use ChatGPT to draft a weekly email series. You write a prompt. The output is close, but the tone is off — reads like a bank pamphlet. You rewrite the prompt, try again. Still off, in a different way. After six tries you're not sure what changed or why any of them worked.

a_state.quote: When the output isn't right, I rewrite the prompt and hope it gets better.

b_state.quote: I isolate what's broken, change one variable at a time, and log what I learn.

objectives: Diagnose which part of a prompt is causing a bad output instead of rewriting the whole thing.
Run a controlled test comparing two prompt variations to learn what actually moves the needle.
Keep a simple debug log so you build a personal library of what works and can hand it to an AI developer.
scenario.why_it_matters: Roles that collaborate with AI developers to refine content generation expect you to bring a systematic debugging practice — not just faster typing.

concepts.title: The IVL method: three moves, in order
concepts.intro: Most people at Level 2 treat a bad output as a signal to rewrite the whole prompt. Level 3 treats it as a diagnostic puzzle. Here are the three moves that make the difference.

cards.headline: Find the one piece that's broken.
cards.body: A prompt has parts: role, task, context, examples, format, constraints. When output is off, one of those parts is usually the culprit — not the whole prompt.
cards.analogy: Like editing a paragraph: you find the weak sentence, not rewrite the whole page.

cards.headline: Change one variable. Keep the rest constant.
cards.body: If you change three things at once and the output improves, you don't know which change worked. Change one, compare the output, then move on.
cards.analogy: Like testing a recipe: don't swap the flour and the oven temp in the same batch.

cards.headline: Write down what you tried and what happened.
cards.body: Memory lies. A simple log (prompt version, what changed, quality 1-5, note) turns 'I'm fiddling' into 'I'm learning.' It's also what separates L2 from L3.
cards.analogy: Like a writer's notebook: you don't remember every good line, you capture it.

concepts.pull_quote: If you only remember one thing: change one variable at a time.

example_1.title: Debugging a tone problem
example_1.setup: The client wants warm, human financial-wellness emails. Your first draft prompt gets output that reads like a bank pamphlet. Let's debug it.

original_prompt.label: The original prompt (v1)
original_prompt.prompt: Write a weekly email to subscribers about building an emergency fund. Make it friendly.
original_prompt.output: Dear Valued Subscriber, An emergency fund is a critical financial instrument that provides stability during unexpected circumstances. Experts recommend saving 3–6 months of expenses...
original_prompt.diagnosis: Reads like a bank pamphlet. The word 'friendly' is doing all the work — and ChatGPT doesn't know what friendly means to this brand.

iterated_prompt.label: The iterated prompt (v2)
iterated_prompt.prompt: Write a weekly email to subscribers about building an emergency fund. Match the tone of this sample: 'Hey — real talk. Life throws curveballs, and an emergency fund keeps a flat tire from becoming a financial crisis.' Keep it conversational, use 'you,' avoid words like 'instrument' or 'strategically.'
iterated_prompt.output: Hey — let's talk about the one thing that separates 'I've got this' from 'I'm panicking at 2am': an emergency fund. The good news? You don't need thousands to start. Even $20 a week is a real safety net in 6 months...
iterated_prompt.diagnosis: Big improvement. One variable changed — tone guidance — and the fix landed.

steps.title: Vary one thing: replace 'friendly' with a concrete tone sample
Log it: what did you learn?

log_entries.value: replaced 'friendly' with sample paragraph + banned words
abstract tone words don't work. Show, don't tell.
paste brand sample into every copywriting prompt from now on

example_1.wrap_up: One variable changed, one lesson captured. That's an L3 move.

example_2.kicker: Example two — closer to real work.
example_2.title: A/B testing two prompt variations
example_2.setup: Same task, same information, two different ways to structure the prompt. Which one wins? This is L3 in the wild: run the test, compare, log the lesson.

steps.title: The test setup
steps.commentary: Does putting the role before the task produce better output than putting it after? One variable changed. Everything else identical.

steps.title: Pick a variant to inspect
steps.commentary: Tap each variant to see its output and rating.

comparison.test_question: Does role placement (before vs. after task) affect output quality?

variants.label: Variant A — Role first
variants.prompt: You are a fitness copywriter who writes for busy parents. Write a 40-word product description for a noise-cancelling yoga mat. Emphasize: durability, quiet use (kids sleeping), 60-day trial.
variants.output: Yoga on your schedule — even when the baby's finally down. Our noise-cancelling mat grips quietly on any floor, holds up to daily use, and comes with a 60-day trial. No creaks. No excuses. Just ten stolen minutes.
variants.why: The role ('fitness copywriter for busy parents') primes the model with audience and voice before the task. Result reads like a human wrote it.

variants.label: Variant B — Task first
variants.prompt: Write a 40-word product description for a noise-cancelling yoga mat. Emphasize: durability, quiet use (kids sleeping), 60-day trial. Target audience: busy parents.
variants.output: Introducing our premium noise-cancelling yoga mat — designed for durability and quiet use, so you can exercise without waking the kids. Built to last, backed by a 60-day trial. Perfect for busy parents who need a reliable fitness solution.
variants.why: Same information, different order. Without role framing, the model defaults to generic product-catalog voice.

comparison.takeaway: Prime voice before asking for work. Role-first placement wins for voicey copy.

example_2.wrap_up: You ran a controlled A/B test. One variable. Two outputs. A reusable lesson.

agent_build.title: Build your Prompt Debug Coach
agent_build.intro: Here's your takeaway artifact. It's a custom GPT instruction set that turns ChatGPT into a debugging partner — one that forces the IVL method on you every time. No code. Copy, paste, personalize three fields.

capability_chips.title: Asks for a rating
capability_chips.description: Before diagnosing, it asks you to score the output 1-5.

capability_chips.title: Suggests ONE change
capability_chips.description: Blocks you from changing three things at once.

capability_chips.title: Logs your lessons
capability_chips.description: Formats your learnings into a reusable log.

personalization_fields.label: Your content area
personalization_fields.placeholder: e.g., financial wellness emails for first-time savers

personalization_fields.label: Your brand tone (2-3 sentence sample)
personalization_fields.placeholder: e.g., Hey — real talk. Life throws curveballs...

agent_build.system_prompt_template:
You are my Prompt Debug Coach. You help me systematically improve prompts for ChatGPT using the IVL method.

When I paste in a prompt and its output, you will:

1. ISOLATE — identify which part of the prompt is likely causing the issue. Check: role, task clarity, audience, tone, format, constraints.

2. VARY — suggest exactly ONE change to test. Not three. One. Explain why this variable.

3. LOG — after I share the new output, help me record a one-line lesson: '[What I changed] → [quality change 1-5] → [what I learned]'

Rules:
• Ask me for the output quality (1-5) before diagnosing.
• Never rewrite the whole prompt.
• If I try to change multiple things at once, push back.
• After 5 lessons, suggest a 'patterns recap.'

My domain: {topic}
My brand tone: {tone}
Words I never use: {avoid}
usage_steps: Open a new ChatGPT conversation and paste the prompt above as your first message.
Next time you have a prompt that isn't working, paste the prompt and the bad output.
Answer its 1-5 rating question. Follow its ONE suggested change.
Paste the new output. Log the lesson in Notion, a Google Doc, or a notes app.
After 5 lessons, ask it: 'What patterns do you see across my tests?'

final_affirmation.rubric_quote: I isolate what's broken, change one variable at a time, and log what I learn.
final_affirmation.tie_back: That's the rubric for Level 3 Practitioner on SK.PRM.003. Two weeks of logs gives you the kind of artifact an AI developer wants to see when you apply for content-engineering roles.
